title: Executive Order 9969—Suspension of Eight-hour Law as to Work by the Alaska Railroad, Department of the Interior

flags.justification: A narrow exercise of a specific, statute-provided emergency-suspension exception, bounded to one railroad, one seasonal rehabilitation purpose, and a defined five-month period.
The Secretary of the Interior must make a specific finding that suspension is essential for each particular work, a real case-by-case check rather than a blanket suspension.
This suspends an existing labor-hour restriction rather than creating a new compliance regime.
No compliance regime exists for an incentive structure to attach to.
An explicit, specific five-month duration, effective immediately.

The order substitutes one protective mechanism for another rather than bare removal: the daily eight-hour cap is suspended, but wages are guaranteed on a forty-hour administrative workweek with time-and-a-half overtime for hours beyond that — a genuinely well-designed protective substitution suited to the seasonal-rehabilitation context.
The overtime-pay guarantee is a real, textually explicit worker protection, not left to later administrative discretion.
Narrow and bounded in scope (one railroad, one five-month period, a specific rehabilitation purpose) despite invoking national-security framing in the preamble.
No second agency's competing mandate is implicated.
A narrow, bounded exemption from the Eight-Hour Law for a specific purpose and duration, paired with a real compensating protection rather than an undermining carve-out.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 0/16 = 0.0%. A genuinely well-designed order: rather than simply removing a worker protection, it substitutes a more contextually appropriate one (weekly overtime pay in place of a daily hour cap) for a specific, bounded, seasonal purpose.
